title: Graph automorphisms to obtain Clifford symmetries in open and closed qudit models
authors: Charlie Nation, Rick P. A. Simon, Shreya Banerjee, Francesco Martini, Alessandro Ricottone, Federico Cerisola, Luca Dellantonio
abstract: In the recent article [arXiv:2605.18966], we demonstrated that finding Clifford symmetries can be mapped to a Graph Automorphism (GA) problem. Here, we provide an algorithm to obtain such symmetries on general qudit systems, that works on the principle of encoding Clifford invariants of a Hamiltonian onto properties of a graph. Labelling Hamiltonian terms as vertices, a permutation of such vertices that respects the Clifford invariants (a GA) is both a valid Clifford, and a symmetry up to phase correction checks. We test this on multiple physical models and discuss the scaling with respect to the number of qudits and Pauli strings, as well as various strategies for optimisation in different regimes. We further show that the graph automorphism representation of Clifford symmetries can be expanded to open quantum systems.
summary: This paper develops a general algorithmic approach to find Clifford symmetries in qudit systems by reformulating the problem as a Graph Automorphism (GA) problem. It constructs a graph based on Pauli commutation invariants and uses advanced search heuristics like WL refinement and MRV to find the symmetries. The method is robust enough to handle both closed Hamiltonians and open Liouvillian dynamics.
main_problem: The paper addresses the theoretical challenge of finding Clifford symmetries for quantum systems, which is mapped to a computationally hard Graph Automorphism (GA) problem.
main_result: An algorithm is presented that successfully finds these symmetries for general qudit systems, extending the formalism from closed Hamiltonians to open Liouvillian systems.
method: The core method involves constructing a colored, edge-labeled graph based on the Pauli commutation relations and encoding the symmetry search as finding a graph automorphism that preserves these invariants.
model_or_system: The work applies to general qudit systems, covering both closed systems described by Hamiltonians (e.g., Toric Code, TFI) and open systems governed by GKSL master equations.
key_observables: Clifford symmetries ($\hat{S}$), graph automorphisms ($\Pi$), Pauli commutation relations ($\langle \vec{p}_i, \vec{p}_j angle$), and phase correction vectors ($\vec{\phi}$).
important_parameters: The local dimension $d$ of the qudits, the number of qudits $n$, and the structure of the Hamiltonian/Liouvillian.
main_assumptions: The search relies on mapping the symmetry problem to GA, which is computationally intensive, and the phase correction step requires careful handling beyond the pure graph structure.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the time scaling of symmetry finding for various models (Random Pauli, TFI, Heisenberg XXZ, Toric Code) comparing different search heuristics (MRV vs. bliss).
paper_structure: The paper builds from establishing the GA mapping for symmetries, detailing the graph construction using Pauli invariants, refining the search using WL coloring and MRV heuristics, and finally extending the formalism to open systems and specific physical examples like the ladder structure.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, general algorithmic framework for characterizing symmetries in complex quantum models, which is crucial for understanding conserved quantities and simplifying the analysis of quantum dynamics in both closed and open settings.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Time taken to find Clifford symmetries on the models in Appendix F. (a) Random Pauli Hamiltonian with an injected SWAP (blue) and RCC (yellow, see main text) symmetry. (b) TFI model on differing geometries for open and closed systems. (c) Fermionic models after Jordan-Wigner transformation (see Appendix F): the disordered tV ladder (blue), and a Fermi-Hubbard chain (yellow). (d) Heisenberg XXZ with local fields on each site for a ladder (yellow) and all-to-all connected (blue) geometries. Scaling with number of Paulis M shown in inset. (e) Highly symmetric Toric code (cg = 0) for d = 2, 3, comparing bliss method to MRV search in Appendix E. (f) Toric code (cg , cz , cx), comparing...
